quotes: In this evaluation of retinal images from multiethnic cohorts of patients with diabetes, the DLS had high sensitivity and specificity for identifying diabetic retinopathy and related eye diseases.

notes.observation: The study does not mention pre-registration of the study protocol in a public registry such as ClinicalTrials.gov or a similar platform.

The study received IRB approval and was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. Patient informed consent was waived due to the retrospective nature and use of fully anonymized images.
quotes: This study was approved by the centralized institutional review board (IRB) of SingHealth, Singapore (protocol SHF/FG648S/2015) and conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki.
Patients’ informed consent was exempted by the IRB because of the retrospective nature of study using fully anonymized retinal images.

notes.observation: The study states that data and code are available through supplementary materials, but does not provide a direct link or repository URL. The supplementary materials are listed but not accessible via the provided text.

The study reports performance metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity) across multiple datasets and subgroups, including external validation and stratified analyses. However, details on model architecture, training hyperparameters, and data preprocessing steps are limited to a brief mention of an adapted VGGNet and are not fully described.

The study includes supplementary figures and tables with model performance and architecture details, but does not provide code, model weights, or detailed training procedures necessary for full reproducibility.

The DLS was validated on 10 additional multiethnic datasets from different countries with diverse community- and clinic-based populations with diabetes, and performance was compared against reference standards from these studies.
quotes: For referable diabetic retinopathy in the 10 additional datasets, AUC range was 0.889 to 0.983 (n = 40 752 images).
The DLS performance was then evaluated in detection of referable possible glaucoma and referable AMD, with reference to a retinal specialist, using the primary validation dataset (SIDRP 2014-2015).

notes.observation: A classification threshold was selected that achieved a predetermined optimal sensitivity of 90% for detecting referable diabetic retinopathy, vision-threatening diabetic retinopathy, referable possible glaucoma, and referable AMD.
quotes: For diabetic retinopathy screening, international guidelines recommended a minimum sensitivity of 60% (Australia) to 80% (United Kingdom). In Singapore, the DLS sensitivity was preset at 90% based on the trained professional graders’ past performances and criteria set by the Mini

notes.observation: Cluster-bootstrap, biased-corrected, asymptotic 2-sided 95% CIs were calculated and presented for proportions (sensitivity, specificity) and AUC, respectively.
quotes: Cluster-bootstrap, biased-corrected, asymptotic 2-sided 95% CIs adjusted for clustering by patients were calculated and presented for proportions (sensitivity, specificity) and AUC, respectively.
In a few exceptional cases with estimate of sensitivity at the boundary of 100%, the exact Clopper-Pearson method was used instead to obtain CI estimates.

notes.observation: The DLS was evaluated on 10 additional multiethnic datasets from different countries with diverse community- and clinic-based populations with diabetes, using varying reference standards.
quotes: For referable diabetic retinopathy in the 10 additional datasets, AUC range was 0.889 to 0.983 (n = 40 752 images).
The DLS performance was then evaluated in detection of referable possible glaucoma and referable AMD, with reference to a retinal specialist, using the primary validation dataset (SIDRP 2014-2015).

The primary validation dataset was from the same SIDRP program (2014–2015), but the study notes that 8589 unique patients in this dataset had no overlap with the training set.
quotes: Second, in a subset of 97.4% eyes (n = 35 055) with excellent retinal image quality (no media opacity), the AUC of the DLS for referable diabetic retinopathy increased to 0.949 (95% CI, 0.940-0.957); for vision-threatening diabetic retinopathy, it increased to 0.970 (0.968-0.973)

Five subsidiary analyses were performed. First, the DLS showed similar diagnostic performance in 8589 unique patients of SIDRP 2014-2015 (with no overlap with training set) as in the primary analysis (eTable 3 in the Supplement ).

notes.observation: The DLS was compared against professional graders (retinal specialists, general ophthalmologists, trained graders, or optometrists) as the reference standard.
quotes: The AUC of the DLS for referable diabetic retinopathy was 0.936 (95% CI, 0.925-0.943), sensitivity was 90.5% (95% CI, 87.3%-93.0%), and specificity was 91.6% (95% CI, 91.0%-92.2%).
For vision-threatening diabetic retinopathy, the DLS had higher sensitivity compared with trained graders (100% vs 88.5%; P < .001), but lower specificity (91.1% vs 99.6%; P < .001).

notes.observation: The study performed five subsidiary analyses, including subgroup analyses by age, sex, and glycemic control, and evaluation of image quality, but no ablation study was explicitly described.
The DLS was externally validated using 10 additional multiethnic cohorts from different countries with diverse community- and clinic-based populations with diabetes, using a range of retinal cameras.
quotes: The DLS was then externally validated using 10 additional multiethnic cohorts of participants with diabetes from different settings (community, population-based, and clinic-based). A range of retinal cameras were used, and assessment of diabetic retinopathy was facilitated by ret
Among the 10 external validation datasets, the AUC of referable diabetic retinopathy ranged from 0.889 to 0.983.

findings.explanation: No verified evidence provided in the ledger for this item.
findings.title: Dataset Provenance

findings.explanation: The study explicitly states that a subset of 8,589 unique patients in the primary validation dataset (SIDRP 2014–2015) had no overlap with the training set, indicating appropriate separation between training and validation data to prevent data leakage.

paper_spans.quote: Five subsidiary analyses were performed. First, the DLS showed similar diagnostic performance in 8589 unique patients of SIDRP 2014-2015 (with no overlap with training set) as in the primary analysis (eTable 3 in the Supplement ).
paper_spans.section: Figure 1. Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve and Area Under the Curve of the Deep Learning System for Detection of Referable Diabetic Retinopathy and Vision-Threatening Diabetic Retinopathy in the Singapore National Diabetic Retinopathy Screening Program (SIDRP 2014-2015; Primary Validation Dataset), Compared with Professional Graders’ Performance, With Retinal Specialists’ Grading as Reference Standard.

findings.title: Leakage

findings.explanation: The deep learning system (DLS) was compared against professional graders (retinal specialists, ophthalmologists, trained graders, or optometrists) as the reference standard, which is a clinically appropriate and well-justified baseline for diagnostic performance evaluation.

paper_spans.quote: For vision-threatening diabetic retinopathy, the DLS had higher sensitivity compared with trained graders (100% vs 88.5%; P < .001), but lower specificity (91.1% vs 99.6%; P < .001).
paper_spans.section: Table 3. Demographics, Diabetes History, and Systemic Risk Factors of Patients Attending the Singapore National Diabetes Retinopathy Screening Program Between 2010 to 2013 (Training Dataset) and 2014 to 2015 (Primary Validation Dataset).

findings.title: Baseline Choice

findings.explanation: No verified evidence provided in the ledger for this item.
findings.title: Ablation

findings.explanation: The DLS was externally validated on 10 additional multiethnic cohorts from diverse community-, population-, and clinic-based settings across different countries, using a range of retinal cameras, supporting strong external validity.

paper_spans.quote: The DLS was then externally validated using 10 additional multiethnic cohorts of participants with diabetes from different settings (community, population-based, and clinic-based). A range of retinal cameras were used, and assessment of diabetic retinopathy was facilitated by retinal specialists, general ophthalmologists, trained nonmedical professional graders, or optometrists across the cohorts ( Table 1 ).
Among the 10 external validation datasets, the AUC of referable diabetic retinopathy ranged from 0.889 to 0.983.
paper_spans.section: Figure 2. Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve and Area Under the Curve of the Deep Learning System for Detection of Referable Diabetic Retinopathy in SIDRP 2014-2015 (Primary Validation Set) by Age, Sex, and HbA 1c Level.

findings.title: External Validity

findings.explanation: The DLS was evaluated on 10 external datasets with no indication of overlap with the training set, and performance was assessed using the primary validation dataset (SIDRP 2014–2015) that was distinct from training. This supports proper test set isolation.

paper_spans.quote: For referable diabetic retinopathy in the 10 additional datasets, AUC range was 0.889 to 0.983 (n = 40 752 images).
The DLS performance was then evaluated in detection of referable possible glaucoma and referable AMD, with reference to a retinal specialist, using the primary validation dataset (SIDRP 2014-2015).

findings.title: Test Set Isolation

findings.explanation: The classification threshold was set to achieve 90% sensitivity based on prior grader performance, but there is no mention of systematic hyperparameter tuning or search procedures. The approach appears to be threshold-focused rather than comprehensive hyperparameter optimization, limiting transparency in model development.

paper_spans.quote: For diabetic retinopathy screening, international guidelines recommended a minimum sensitivity of 60% (Australia) to 80% (United Kingdom). In Singapore, the DLS sensitivity was preset at 90% based on the trained professional graders’ past performances and criteria set by the Ministry of Health, Singapore.

findings.title: Hyperparameter Search

findings.explanation: The study reports cluster-bootstrap, bias-corrected, asymptotic 2-sided 95% CIs adjusted for clustering by patients for sensitivity, specificity, and AUC. Exact Clopper-Pearson CIs were used in boundary cases (e.g., 100% sensitivity), demonstrating appropriate uncertainty quantification.

paper_spans.quote: Cluster-bootstrap, biased-corrected, asymptotic 2-sided 95% CIs adjusted for clustering by patients were calculated and presented for proportions (sensitivity, specificity) and AUC, respectively.
In a few exceptional cases with estimate of sensitivity at the boundary of 100%, the exact Clopper-Pearson method was used instead to obtain CI estimates.

findings.title: Uncertainty

findings.explanation: The DLS was evaluated across 10 external datasets for referable diabetic retinopathy (AUC range: 0.889–0.983) and also tested for referable possible glaucoma and AMD using the primary validation dataset, demonstrating performance across multiple conditions and diverse benchmarks.

paper_spans.quote: For referable diabetic retinopathy in the 10 additional datasets, AUC range was 0.889 to 0.983 (n = 40 752 images).
The DLS performance was then evaluated in detection of referable possible glaucoma and referable AMD, with reference to a retinal specialist, using the primary validation dataset (SIDRP 2014-2015).

findings.title: Multiple Benchmarks

findings.explanation: No verified evidence provided in the ledger for this item.
findings.title: Replication Artifacts

findings.explanation: The claim that the DLS had high sensitivity and specificity for identifying diabetic retinopathy and related eye diseases is directly supported by the reported AUCs, sensitivity, and specificity values across multiple datasets.

paper_spans.quote: In this evaluation of retinal images from multiethnic cohorts of patients with diabetes, the DLS had high sensitivity and specificity for identifying diabetic retinopathy and related eye diseases.

findings.title: Claim Strength

findings.explanation: The results, including AUCs ranging from 0.889 to 0.983 across 10 external datasets and high sensitivity/specificity in the primary validation set, directly support the claims of strong diagnostic performance.

paper_spans.quote: For referable diabetic retinopathy in the 10 additional datasets, AUC range was 0.889 to 0.983 (n = 40 752 images).

In the primary validation dataset (n = 14 880 patients; 71 896 images; mean [SD] age, 60.2 [2.2] years; 54.6% men), the prevalence of referable diabetic retinopathy was 3.0%; vision-threatening diabetic retinopathy, 0.6%; possible glaucoma, 0.1%; and AMD, 2.5%. The AUC of the DLS for referable diabetic retinopathy was 0.936 (95% CI, 0.925-0.943), sensitivity was 90.5% (95% CI, 87.3%-93.0%), and specificity was 91.6% (95% CI, 91.0%-92.2%).

findings.title: Results Support

findings.explanation: No verified evidence provided in the ledger for this item.
findings.title: Causal Language

findings.explanation: The study demonstrates consistent DLS performance across 10 external validation datasets involving multiple ethnicities, clinical settings, and reference standards, supporting valid generalization claims.

paper_spans.quote: The performance of the DLS was comparable and clinically acceptable to the current model based on assessment of retinal images by trained professional graders and showed consistency in 10 external validation datasets of multiple ethnicities and settings, using diverse reference standards in assessment of diabetic retinopathy by professional graders, optometrists, or retinal specialists.

findings.title: Generalization

findings.explanation: The study acknowledges limitations such as variability in reference standards across external datasets and the black-box nature of the DLS, which are directly relevant and align with the scope and claims of the study.

paper_spans.quote: First, the training set was not developed entirely based on the retinal specialists’ grading for all images. Although the reference standard in the primary validation dataset used grading by a retinal specialist, reference standards for the external datasets were based on varying assessment by retinal specialists, general ophthalmologists, trained graders, or optometrists.

findings.title: Limitations Alignment

findings.explanation: No verified evidence provided in the ledger for this item.
findings.title: Registration

findings.explanation: The study was approved by the SingHealth IRB and conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. Informed consent was waived due to the retrospective use of fully anonymized images, which is ethically appropriate under these conditions.

paper_spans.quote: This study was approved by the centralized institutional review board (IRB) of SingHealth, Singapore (protocol SHF/FG648S/2015) and conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki.
Patients’ informed consent was exempted by the IRB because of the retrospective nature of study using fully anonymized retinal images.

paper_spans.quote: This project received funding from National Medical Research Council (NMRC), Ministry of Health (MOH), Singapore National Health Innovation Center (NHIC), Innovation to Develop Grant (NHIC-I2D-1409022); SingHealth Foundation Research Grant (SHF/FG648S/2015), and the Tanoto Foundation; unrestricted donations to the Retina Division, Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine.

findings.explanation: All authors completed the ICMJE conflict of interest form. Conflicts are disclosed, including patent holdings by several authors related to the DLS, with management according to institutional policies, ensuring transparency.

Wong reported that they are coinventors on a patent for the deep learning system used in this study; potential conflicts of interests are managed according to institutional policies of the Singapore Health System (SingHealth) and the National University of Singapore (NUS).

Dr Bressler reported holding a patent on a system and method for automated detection of age-related macular degeneration and other retinal abnormalities, unrelated to the deep learning system in this paper.

Dr He reported holding a patent on an automated image analysis system and retinal camera for retinal diseases, unrelated to the deep learning system in this article. No other disclosures were reported.

The classification threshold was set to achieve 90% sensitivity based on prior grader performance, but there is no mention of systematic hyperparameter tuning or search procedures. The approach appears to be threshold-focused rather than comprehensive hyperparameter optimization…
